title: Fourier interpolation on the real line

thm:1.summary: Even Fourier interpolation on $\mathbb{R}$: there exists a basis of even Schwartz functions $\{a_n\}_{n\ge 0}$ such that every even Schwartz $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ is reconstructed from $f(\sqrt n)$ and $\widehat{f}(\sqrt n)$, $n\ge 0$ — the analytic skeleton behind Viazovska's magic functions, abstracted to a free-standing interpolation theorem.

cor:1.summary: Vanishing principle: an even Schwartz $f$ with $f(\sqrt n)=\widehat{f}(\sqrt n)=0$ for all $n\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$ vanishes identically — the rigidity statement that pins down the magic function up to scalars.

thm:2.summary: The interpolation map $\Psi$ is an isomorphism between even Schwartz functions and $\ker L\subset\mathfrak{s}\oplus\mathfrak{s}$ for an explicit linear functional $L$ — characterizes which value/transform-value sequences come from a Schwartz function.

thm:3.summary: Modular generating functions for the even interpolation basis: $\sum_{n\ge 0} g_n^{\pm}(z)e^{i\pi n\tau} = \theta(\tau)(1-2\lambda(\tau))\cdots$ — explicit weight-$3/2$ weakly-holomorphic modular forms on $\Gamma_\theta$ yielding the basis $\{a_n\}$.

thm:4.summary: Growth bound on the even basis: $|b_n^\varepsilon(x)| = O(n^2)$ uniformly in $x$ — the polynomial-growth estimate that lets the interpolation series converge for Schwartz inputs.

thm:5.summary: Modular generating functions for the odd interpolation basis: $\sum_{n\ge 0} h_n^{\pm}(z)e^{i\pi n\tau} = \theta^3(\tau)(1-2\lambda(\tau))\cdots$ — the odd analogue of Theorem 3.

thm:6.summary: Growth bound on the odd basis: $|d_n^\varepsilon(x)| = O(n^{5/2})$ uniformly in $x$ — odd analogue of Theorem 4 with the slightly weaker exponent.

thm:7.summary: Odd Fourier interpolation on $\mathbb{R}$: the odd analogue of Theorem 1 — an interpolation basis for odd Schwartz functions from values at $\sqrt n$, $n\ge 1$.

prop:1.summary: $b_m^\varepsilon$ is a Schwartz eigenfunction of the Fourier transform: $\widehat{b_m^\varepsilon}(x) = \varepsilon\, b_m^\varepsilon(x)$ and satisfies the prescribed interpolation values — the building block of the even basis.

prop:2.summary: $F_\varepsilon(\tau,x)$ admits an analytic continuation to the upper half-plane $\mathfrak{H}$ satisfying the prescribed functional equation — converts the interpolation problem into a modular-form question.

prop:3.summary: $d_m^\varepsilon$ is an odd Schwartz function and a Fourier eigenfunction with eigenvalue $-i\varepsilon$ — the odd analogue of $b_m^\varepsilon$.

prop:4.summary: Structural property of the odd interpolation basis (analogue of prop:1 / prop:2 for the odd case) used in deducing Theorem 7.

lem:1.summary: Fourier-expansion growth bound: a $2$-periodic analytic $f(\tau)=\sum_{n\ge 0}a_n e^{i\pi n\tau}$ with $|f(\tau)|\le C\,\mathrm{Im}(\tau)^{-\alpha}$ has $|a_n|\le C' n^\alpha$ — the modular-form analogue of Bessel's inequality used in the growth bound.

lem:2.summary: Theta-group iteration: for $\tau\in\mathfrak{H}$ with $|\tau|\ge 1$ and a sequence of nonzero integers, defines an iterated sequence $\tau_j\in\mathfrak{H}$ moving toward the cusp — the elementary geometric input to the growth estimate.

lem:3.summary: Cocycle decay lemma: for a cocycle $\{\psi_A\}_{A\in\Gamma_\theta}$ relative to the slash action $|_{k/2}^{-\varepsilon}$ with $\psi_{T^2}=0$ and an explicit bound on $\psi_S$, controls the growth of $\psi$ across the theta group.

lem:4.summary: Derivative-and-moment bound: $|x^k \frac{d^n}{dx^n}F_\varepsilon(\tau,x)| \le C_{n,k}(1+\mathrm{Im}(\tau)^{-(n+k+1)/2})$ — the Schwartz-class control needed to interchange limits in the interpolation formula.

edges.note: The interpolation basis $a_n$ is assembled from the Schwartz eigenfunctions $b_m^\varepsilon$ of Proposition 1.
The basis $\{a_n\}$ is defined via the modular generating functions of Theorem 3.
Convergence of the interpolation series requires the polynomial growth bound (Theorem 4).
Vanishing is the kernel statement of the interpolation isomorphism (Theorem 1).
The isomorphism statement specifies the image of the interpolation map of Theorem 1.
The generating-function identity rests on the analytic continuation of $F_\varepsilon$ (Proposition 2).
The eigenfunction property is read off the analytic continuation and functional equation of $F_\varepsilon$.
The growth bound uses the Fourier-expansion / Bessel-type lemma.
Theta-group iteration is the geometric input to the growth estimate.
The cocycle decay lemma controls growth across $\Gamma_\theta$.
Derivative-and-moment control on $F_\varepsilon$ closes the growth argument.
The odd interpolation theorem rests on the odd eigenfunctions $d_m^\varepsilon$ (Proposition 3).
The odd basis is assembled from the odd generating functions of Theorem 5.
Convergence requires the odd growth bound (Theorem 6).
The structural odd-basis property of Proposition 4 enters the deduction.
The odd generating functions encode the odd eigenfunctions of Proposition 3.
Odd growth uses the same Fourier-expansion bound.
Odd growth uses the derivative-and-moment control.

main_theorems.description: The interpolation theorems for even and odd Schwartz functions, the vanishing corollary, and the isomorphism with $\ker L$.

generating_functions.description: Explicit weakly-holomorphic modular forms on $\Gamma_\theta$ whose Fourier coefficients give the even and odd interpolation bases.

eigenfunctions.description: Schwartz Fourier-eigenfunctions $b_m^\varepsilon$ and $d_m^\varepsilon$ that the bases are built from, and their analytic-continuation backbone.

growth_bounds.description: Polynomial growth bounds $O(n^2)$ on the even basis and $O(n^{5/2})$ on the odd basis — what makes the interpolation series converge.

modular_lemmas.description: Auxiliary modular-form / theta-group lemmas (Fourier-expansion bound, theta-group iteration, cocycle decay, derivative-and-moment control) used in the growth estimates.

Viazovska-E8.title: The sphere packing problem in dimension 8 (Viazovska)

Leech.title: The sphere packing problem in dimension 24 (CKMRV)
